title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
summary:
Hercule Poirot and his companion, Kattimer, use a universal key to inspect the rooms of the three victims: Harriet Sipper (room 121), Richard Nigges (room 238), and Ada Gransbury (room 317). Poirot observes that the victims' rooms are located on consecutive floors and concludes they were not randomly selected, noting that all three checked into the hotel on the same day and share a connection to the initials "PIJ."

The rooms are nearly identical, containing standard furniture and the corpses of the victims. The bodies are meticulously arranged on the floor with their arms at their sides and their feet facing the door, a positioning Kattimer notes resembles how doctors arrange deceased patients. In the rooms of Sipper and Gransbury, teacups and saucers are present on coffee tables. However, Gransbury’s room also contains a tray with an extra teacup and crumbs, suggesting she may have had dinner with someone. In contrast, Nigges’ room contains only a sherry glass. Poirot also notes that Gransbury brought significantly more clothing than the other victims.

Upon returning to Nigges' room, Poirot identifies several discrepancies. Unlike the other rooms, Nigges' window is wide open, making the room cold, and the view overlooks the hotel garden rather than "Great London." Furthermore, Poirot observes a difference in the placement of the cufflinks: while the cufflinks in Sipper's and Gransbury's mouths protrude slightly, Nigges' cufflink is pushed deep into his throat. Kattimer attempts to dismiss this as a minor detail, arguing that the shared initials are the primary commonality.
